body_txt: A murderer is attacking a family. Both parents are killed onscreen, but the child's death is only implied. There may be a scream, but no shot of the death taking place. This is especially common in Hollywood and many Western films, due to the heavy taboos surrounding deaths of children (and that's with Death of a Child ; onscreen child deaths have it even worse). Even in violent movies, any child deaths usually occur offscreen. This is often because onscreen deaths would further upset the Moral Guardians or result in censorship from the Media Watchdogs . And killing a child—even by accident—is commonly seen as a one-way ticket to the Moral Event Horizon . A subtrope of Discretion Shot , Killed Offscreen , and Death of a Child . Compare and contrast Improbable Infant Survival , where the same issues surrounding child death are avoided by having them survive instead. Related to Empathy Doll Shot , wherein a child's favorite toy is presumably all that's left of them. See also Abuse Discretion Shot , in which abuse (including child abuse) doesn't get shown onscreen. Note: This trope mainly concerns preteens (age 12 and under), though it can also apply to younger teenagers if the handling of their deaths contrasts the deaths of adults. And this is a Death Trope , so expect unmarked spoilers.
